figures.caption: Figure 3: An example of skull stripping using FSL BET. The left-hand side image shows the original T1-weighted MRI with the skull and non-brain tissues. The right-hand side image shows the result after applying FSL BET. The brain region is cleanly extracted and the surrounding skull is removed.
Figure 8: Segmentation result from the sagittal model on a sagittal slice.
Figure 9: Segmentation result from the unified model on an axial slice.

Figure 4: An example of tissue segmentation using FSL FAST. The bottom set of images highlights the white matter, while the top set of images highlights the gray matter in the same subject. We have performed the segmentation on skull-stripped images. We have overlaid the tissue boundaries on coronal, sagittal, and axial slices. We have used these maps to construct pixelwise multi-class labels for model training.
Figure 1: Comparison between the original MedSAM model architecture and the modified model architecture.

Figures 6-9 present representative segmentation examples from each trained model. Each figure follows the following layout: the top row displays the original brain MRI slice (left), pseudo ground truth multi-class mask (middle), and model prediction (right). In all masks, red indicates gray matter, green indicates white matter, and black represents background. The bottom row shows predicted class probability maps from background, gray matter, and white matter as heatmaps scaled from 0.0 (darkest) to 1.0 (brightest), where brighter colors indicate higher model confidence.

Figure 2: Training pipeline comparison: single-orientation models are trained on slices from one anatomical plane (axial, sagittal, or coronal), while the unified model is trained on combined data from all three orientations.
